---
stepsCompleted:
  - step-01-validate-prerequisites
  - step-02-design-epics
  - step-03-create-stories
  - step-04-final-validation
inputDocuments:
  - _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/prd.md
  - _bmad-output/planning-artifacts/architecture.md
---

# src - Epic Breakdown

## Overview

This document provides the complete epic and story breakdown for the Python Automation Governance Framework, decomposing the requirements from the PRD and Architecture into implementable stories.

## Requirements Inventory

### Functional Requirements

- **FR1:** Developer can clone a standardized boilerplate template from the repository to start a new script
- **FR2:** Developer can choose between a minimal or full base Docker image for their script
- **FR3:** Developer can place their business logic into the designated `src/` directory without modifying files outside that directory (governance framework code in `framework/` and `config/` directories is read-only to developers)
- **FR4:** Developer can reference Key Vault secrets via environment variables without knowing the underlying credential values
- **FR5:** Developer can define the CronJob schedule (cron expression) for their script in a configuration file
- **FR6:** Developer can document their script in a standardized README containing required sections: Purpose, Schedule, Inputs, Outputs, Owner, and Dependencies
- **FR7:** Developer can push code to Azure DevOps and create a Pull Request for review
- **FR8:** CI pipeline can automatically validate the submitted code (build, security scan, template compliance)
- **FR9:** Reviewer can see CI pipeline results alongside the Pull Request
- **FR10:** Reviewer can validate code against the security checklist: (1) no hardcoded credentials, (2) template structure compliance, (3) structured logging present, (4) error handling with non-zero exit codes, (5) README completeness
- **FR11:** Reviewer can comment on the Pull Request requesting changes
- **FR12:** Reviewer can approve and merge the Pull Request to trigger deployment
- **FR13:** System can reject PRs that contain detected credentials or secrets in code
- **FR14:** System can automatically build a Docker image and push it to Azure Container Registry upon PR merge
- **FR15:** ArgoCD can detect new or updated images and sync deployments to the AKS cluster
- **FR16:** System can execute Python scripts as Kubernetes CronJobs on the defined schedule
- **FR17:** System can execute Python scripts as on-demand Kubernetes Jobs triggered via CLI command (kubectl or ArgoCD)
- **FR18:** System can automatically retry failed jobs with increasing delay between retries (up to 3 retries)
- **FR19:** System can enforce timeout limits per job execution (default 30 minutes, configurable per CronJob up to 4 hours)
- **FR20:** System can enforce resource quotas (CPU, memory) per namespace and per job
- **FR21:** Admin can provision service account credentials in Azure Key Vault per business area
- **FR22:** System can inject Key Vault secrets into CronJob pods at runtime
- **FR23:** System can scope credential access per namespace (logistics scripts cannot access finance secrets)
- **FR24:** System can audit all credential access events — logged to Key Vault audit logs with caller identity, timestamp, and operation type, retained for minimum 90 days
- **FR25:** Operator can view a platform-wide dashboard showing all namespaces, total jobs, and aggregate success rates
- **FR26:** Operator can drill down into a specific namespace to see all CronJobs and their status
- **FR27:** Operator can view individual CronJob execution history (minimum last 100 runs or 90 days, whichever is greater, with duration and status per run)
- **FR28:** Operator can view logs for any specific job execution
- **FR29:** Operator can view duration trends for each CronJob over time
- **FR30:** System can collect and display per-CronJob resource consumption metrics (CPU, memory, duration)
- **FR31:** System can track compute cost (CPU-seconds, memory-seconds) per individual CronJob execution
- **FR32:** System can aggregate costs per namespace (daily, weekly, monthly)
- **FR33:** Operator can view a cost breakdown dashboard per CronJob and per namespace
- **FR34:** Operator can export monthly cost reports per namespace for chargeback in CSV format via Grafana dashboard export
- **FR35:** System can send alerts to Microsoft Teams when a job fails after all retries
- **FR36:** System can send alerts when job execution time exceeds 2x the historical average
- **FR37:** System can route alerts by severity level (Critical → Teams + Email, High → Teams + Email, Medium → Email, Low → Dashboard only)
- **FR38:** Operator can view active alerts and alert history in the dashboard
- **FR39:** Admin can create and configure namespaces per business area
- **FR40:** Admin can map AD security groups to namespace-scoped RBAC roles
- **FR41:** Developer can only view and interact with resources in their own namespace
- **FR42:** Reviewer can view resources across all namespaces (read-only)
- **FR43:** Admin can set and adjust resource quotas per namespace
- **FR44:** Admin can provision AKS clusters for DEV and PRD environments
- **FR45:** Admin can configure ArgoCD to watch specific repository folders per namespace
- **FR46:** System can collect logs centrally via Loki for all job executions across namespaces
- **FR47:** Admin can configure base Docker images and publish them to the Container Registry

### NonFunctional Requirements

- **NFR1:** Zero secrets in code, config files, or environment variable definitions — CI secret scanning (e.g., TruffleHog or CredScan) blocks PRs containing detected credentials, verified on every pull request
- **NFR2:** Key Vault access scoped per namespace — automated test verifies logistics pods cannot retrieve finance secrets, executed on every infrastructure change and weekly scheduled scan
- **NFR3:** 100% of credential access events recorded in Key Vault audit logs — completeness verified monthly by comparing access count against application request count, with < 1% discrepancy tolerance
- **NFR4:** Container images scanned for known vulnerabilities (CVEs) during CI pipeline — zero Critical/High CVEs allowed before push to ACR, verified per build
- **NFR5:** RBAC permissions verified by automated test suite — each namespace role can only access its designated resources, tests run on every infrastructure change and weekly scheduled scan
- **NFR6:** CronJob pods cannot communicate with pods in other namespaces — cross-namespace network isolation verified by automated connectivity tests on every infrastructure change and weekly scheduled scan
- **NFR7:** All API and dashboard access requires corporate AD (SSO) authentication — zero anonymous access paths, verified by automated access audit on every infrastructure change and quarterly penetration test
- **NFR8:** Production CronJob execution availability ≥ 99.5% measured monthly (critical batch jobs: SAP invoice processing, month-end reconciliation)
- **NFR9:** Failed jobs retry automatically up to 3 times with increasing delay between retries — alert sent to Teams within 2 minutes of final retry failure, measured via Grafana alert rule evaluation
- **NFR10:** Mean failure detection time < 5 minutes via Grafana alerting to Teams + Email
- **NFR11:** No single point of failure — cluster maintains workload availability during any single node failure, verified by quarterly chaos test (node drain) with zero missed scheduled CronJob executions
- **NFR12:** ArgoCD self-heals drift — if a CronJob manifest is manually modified in-cluster, ArgoCD reverts to Git-defined state within 3 minutes
- **NFR13:** Key Vault secret rotation does not cause job failures — running jobs continue using valid credentials through rotation events, verified by monthly rotation test with zero job failures during 24-hour observation window
- **NFR14:** Platform supports scaling from pilot (5-10 CronJobs) to full rollout (60+ CronJobs) — verified by load test deploying 60 concurrent CronJobs with all jobs completing within defined timeout and zero resource contention errors
- **NFR15:** AKS cluster supports horizontal node autoscaling (3-10 nodes) based on pending pod demand
- **NFR16:** Each namespace supports up to 20 concurrent CronJobs without resource contention
- **NFR17:** New business area onboarding (namespace + RBAC + Key Vault) completable within 2 hours of manual admin work
- **NFR18:** CronJob pod startup time (image pull + container init) < 60 seconds for cached images, < 120 seconds for cold pull
- **NFR19:** Grafana dashboard page load < 3 seconds for up to 30 days of execution history
- **NFR20:** CI pipeline (build + security scan + push to ACR) completes in < 10 minutes for standard scripts
- **NFR21:** Log query response time (Loki) < 5 seconds for last 7 days of logs per CronJob
- **NFR22:** SAP connectivity from AKS pods verified end-to-end — network path, name resolution, and authentication functional before pilot launch, measured by automated smoke test completing a read operation against SAP within 10 seconds, executed daily
- **NFR23:** ArgoCD sync latency < 5 minutes from merge-to-main to CronJob updated in cluster
- **NFR24:** Grafana alerting delivers Teams notifications within 60 seconds of alert condition trigger
- **NFR25:** Azure DevOps CI pipeline triggers automatically on PR creation and on merge to main — trigger reliability ≥ 99% measured monthly (zero missed triggers per 100 events), verified via pipeline run history audit
- **NFR26:** Prometheus metrics scraping interval ≤ 30 seconds for CronJob execution metrics

### Additional Requirements

**From Architecture — Infrastructure & Platform Decisions:**

- Monorepo structure: `terraform/` for IaC modules, `k8s/` for Kubernetes manifests
- Terraform state backend on Azure Storage Account with RA-GRS replication and per-module state file segmentation (7 state files: networking, aks, security, monitoring, argocd, namespaces, ci)
- VNet peering from AKS VNet to SAP private network + Private DNS zones for SAP hostname resolution
- Default-deny NetworkPolicy per namespace with explicit egress allowlists (Calico or Azure NPM)
- Trivy Operator for continuous in-cluster vulnerability scanning (complements CI-time Trivy scan)
- ArgoCD ApplicationSets with Git generator — auto-discovers new namespace directories in `k8s/apps/*/`
- Image tagging: Git SHA (immutable) + `latest-<env>` mutable tags for ArgoCD Image Updater
- Manual approval gate for DEV → PRD promotion via Azure DevOps Release Pipeline
- OTel Operator auto-instrumentation for Python — zero-code traces sent to Tempo via annotations
- Structured JSON logs via `structlog` with standard fields: timestamp, level, message, cronjob_name, namespace, trace_id, run_id
- Robusta for alert enrichment (pod logs, resource usage, suggested fixes) → Teams/Email sinks
- Azure Storage Account with 3 containers for LGTM backends: `mimir-blocks`, `loki-chunks`, `tempo-traces`
- Workload Identity (User-Assigned Managed Identity per namespace) for zero-trust pod authentication
- Two-tier base Docker images: `python-gov-base:minimal` (~150MB) and `python-gov-base:full` (~500MB)
- Key Vault CSI Driver with SecretProviderClass per namespace, rotationPollInterval: 2m
- Single Grafana instance embedded in Robusta's kube-prometheus-stack connects to Mimir, Loki, and Tempo

**From Architecture — Implementation Sequence (dependency order):**

1. Networking (VNet, subnets, peering, DNS)
2. AKS cluster (requires networking)
3. Security (Key Vault, CSI driver, managed identities — requires AKS)
4. Monitoring (LGTM + Robusta + OTel Operator — requires AKS + storage)
5. ArgoCD (requires AKS + networking)
6. Namespaces (RBAC, quotas, network policies, SecretProviderClass — requires all above)
7. CI (ACR, pipelines, base images — requires AKS + ACR)
8. Boilerplate template (requires all platform services running)

### FR Coverage Map

- FR1: Epic 2 — Clone boilerplate template
- FR2: Epic 2 — Choose base Docker image
- FR3: Epic 2 — Business logic in src/ directory
- FR4: Epic 3 — Key Vault secret references via env vars

- FR13: Epic 4 — Reject PRs with detected credentials
- FR14: Epic 4 — Auto-build Docker image and push to ACR
- FR15: Epic 4 — ArgoCD sync deployments
- FR16: Epic 2 — CronJob scheduled execution
- FR17: Epic 2 — On-demand job execution via CLI
- FR18: Epic 2 — Auto-retry with increasing delay
- FR19: Epic 2 — Timeout enforcement per job
- FR20: Epic 1 — Resource quotas per namespace and job
- FR21: Epic 3 — Key Vault credential provisioning
- FR22: Epic 3 — Secret injection into pods at runtime
- FR23: Epic 3 — Credential scoping per namespace
- FR24: Epic 3 — Credential access audit logging
- FR25: Epic 5 — Platform-wide dashboard
- FR26: Epic 5 — Namespace drill-down view
- FR27: Epic 5 — CronJob execution history
- FR28: Epic 5 — Job execution log viewer
- FR29: Epic 5 — Duration trends visualization
- FR30: Epic 5 — Per-CronJob resource metrics
- FR31: Epic 6 — Per-CronJob cost tracking
- FR32: Epic 6 — Namespace cost aggregation
- FR33: Epic 6 — Cost breakdown dashboard
- FR34: Epic 6 — Monthly CSV export for chargeback
- FR35: Epic 7 — Teams alert on job failure
- FR36: Epic 7 — Anomaly alert (2x average duration)
- FR37: Epic 7 — Severity-based alert routing
- FR38: Epic 7 — Alert dashboard and history
- FR39: Epic 1 — Namespace creation and configuration
- FR40: Epic 1 — AD group to RBAC role mapping
- FR41: Epic 1 — Developer namespace isolation
- FR42: Epic 1 — Reviewer cross-namespace read access
- FR43: Epic 1 — Resource quota management
- FR44: Epic 1 — AKS cluster provisioning (DEV + PRD)
- FR45: Epic 4 — ArgoCD per-namespace folder watching
- FR46: Epic 5 — Loki centralized log collection
- FR47: Epic 2 — Base Docker image management

**Coverage: 47/47 FRs — Zero gaps**

## Epic List

1. **Platform Infrastructure** — Juliano can provision a governed AKS platform with networking, namespace isolation, RBAC, resource quotas, and Azure Container Registry — the foundation all other epics build upon.
   FRs: FR20, FR39, FR40, FR41, FR42, FR43, FR44 | NFRs: NFR5, NFR6, NFR7, NFR11, NFR14, NFR15, NFR16, NFR17, NFR22

2. **Developer Onboarding & Script Execution** — Marcos can onboard his Python script using the boilerplate template and run it reliably as a scheduled CronJob with retries, timeouts, and resource limits.
   FRs: FR1, FR2, FR3, FR5, FR6, FR16, FR17, FR18, FR19, FR47 | NFRs: NFR8, NFR18

3. **Secure Credential Management** — Marcos can securely reference SAP and Azure credentials without knowing secret values. Juliano provisions and scopes secrets per business area with full audit trail.
   FRs: FR4, FR21, FR22, FR23, FR24 | NFRs: NFR1, NFR2, NFR3, NFR6, NFR13

4. **Automated CI/CD & Code Governance** — Marcos submits code via PR, CI validates automatically, Christian reviews and approves, and ArgoCD deploys via GitOps — the complete governance pipeline.
   FRs: FR7, FR8, FR9, FR10, FR11, FR12, FR13, FR14, FR15, FR45 | NFRs: NFR1, NFR4, NFR12, NFR20, NFR23, NFR25

5. **Monitoring, Logging & Observability** — Ana can see execution status and logs from the dashboard without asking IT. Juliano has full platform-wide visibility into all namespaces, execution history, and resource consumption.
   FRs: FR25, FR26, FR27, FR28, FR29, FR30, FR46 | NFRs: NFR19, NFR21, NFR22, NFR26

6. **Cost Tracking & Chargeback** — Ana and Juliano can track compute costs per script and per business area, and export monthly chargeback reports for finance.
   FRs: FR31, FR32, FR33, FR34

7. **Alerting & Incident Response** — Ana and Juliano receive proactive notifications via Teams and Email when jobs fail or behave anomalously, with severity-based routing and full alert history.
   FRs: FR35, FR36, FR37, FR38 | NFRs: NFR9, NFR10, NFR24

---

## Epic 1: Platform Infrastructure

Juliano can provision a governed AKS platform with networking, namespace isolation, RBAC, resource quotas, and Azure Container Registry — the foundation all other epics build upon.

### Story 1.1: Provision Network Infrastructure

As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
I want VNet, subnets, VNet peering to SAP, and Private DNS zones provisioned via Terraform,
So that AKS can securely connect to SAP and Azure services.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** Terraform networking module is applied
**When** the infrastructure is provisioned
**Then** VNet with required subnets exists in the target resource group
**And** VNet peering to SAP private network is established and connected
**And** Private DNS zones resolve SAP hostnames from within the VNet
**And** SAP connectivity smoke test completes a read operation within 10 seconds from within the VNet (NFR22)
**And** Terraform state is stored in `networking/terraform.tfstate`

### Story 1.2: Provision AKS Clusters for DEV and PRD

As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
I want AKS clusters provisioned in DEV and PRD environments with autoscaling node pools,
So that the platform has a reliable execution environment for Python CronJobs.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** networking infrastructure from Story 1.1 is provisioned
**When** Terraform AKS module is applied for each environment
**Then** AKS cluster is operational in DEV environment
**And** AKS cluster is operational in PRD environment
**And** Node pools support autoscaling from 3 to 10 nodes
**And** Cluster maintains workload availability during single node failure with zero missed CronJob executions (NFR11)
**And** Azure Container Registry (ACR) is provisioned and accessible from both AKS clusters
**And** Terraform state is stored in `aks/terraform.tfstate`

### Story 1.3: Configure Namespace Isolation with RBAC and Quotas

As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
I want namespaces with AD-group-based RBAC, resource quotas, and default-deny network policies,
So that business areas are isolated and resource consumption is controlled.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** AKS cluster is operational
**When** Terraform namespace module is applied for `logistica` namespace
**Then** namespace exists with resource quotas (CPU: 2 req/4 limit cores, Memory: 2/4 Gi)
**And** GRP-PYTHON-DEV AD group is mapped to developer role (view pods, logs in own namespace only)
**And** GRP-PYTHON-REVIEW AD group has read-only access across all namespaces
**And** GRP-PYTHON-OPS AD group can view pods, logs, exec across all areas
**And** GRP-PYTHON-ADMIN AD group has full cluster admin access
**And** default-deny NetworkPolicy blocks cross-namespace pod communication
**And** explicit egress allows DNS, SAP endpoints, and Azure services only

---

## Epic 2: Developer Onboarding & Script Execution

Marcos can onboard his Python script using the boilerplate template and run it reliably as a scheduled CronJob with retries, timeouts, and resource limits.

### Story 2.1: Build and Publish Base Docker Images

As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
I want standardized minimal and full base Docker images published to Azure Container Registry,
So that developers have secure, consistent Python runtimes to build upon.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** ACR is provisioned
**When** base images are built and pushed
**Then** `python-gov-base:minimal` exists in ACR with Python 3.13, logging framework, Key Vault SDK, Prometheus client (~150MB)
**And** `python-gov-base:full` exists in ACR with pandas, numpy, openpyxl, requests, httpx, SAP libraries, sqlalchemy (~500MB)
**And** both images pass Trivy vulnerability scan with zero Critical/High CVEs
**And** images are tagged with build date and `latest`

### Story 2.2: Create CronJob Boilerplate Template

As a Business Developer (Marcos),
I want a boilerplate template with project structure, structured logging, schedule config, and README template,
So that I can onboard my Python script to the platform with minimal friction.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** the boilerplate repository exists in Azure DevOps
**When** a developer clones the template
**Then** `src/` directory exists for business logic with `executar()` entry point
**And** `framework/` and `config/` directories contain read-only governance code
**And** Dockerfile references a base image choice (minimal or full)
**And** `config.yaml` includes a cron schedule expression field
**And** `structlog` is pre-configured with standard fields (timestamp, level, message, cronjob_name, namespace, trace_id, run_id)
**And** README template includes required sections: Purpose, Schedule, Inputs, Outputs, Owner, Dependencies
**And** developer can run the template locally for testing

### Story 2.3: Deploy and Execute Scheduled CronJobs

As a Business Developer (Marcos),
I want my script to run as a Kubernetes CronJob on the defined schedule with retries and timeouts,
So that it executes reliably without my intervention.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** a script is deployed to a namespace as a CronJob
**When** the cron schedule triggers
**Then** the CronJob executes on the defined schedule
**And** failed jobs retry automatically up to 3 times with increasing delay
**And** jobs enforce a timeout limit (default 30 minutes, configurable up to 4 hours)
**And** per-job resource limits are enforced (CPU: 250m req/1 core limit, Memory: 256Mi req/1Gi limit)
**And** pod startup time is < 60 seconds for cached images
**And** production CronJob execution availability is >= 99.5% measured monthly (NFR8)

### Story 2.4: Execute On-Demand Jobs via CLI

As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
I want to trigger job execution on-demand via kubectl or ArgoCD,
So that I can test scripts or run ad-hoc executions outside the schedule.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** a CronJob is deployed in a namespace
**When** an admin runs `kubectl create job --from=cronjob/<name>` or triggers via ArgoCD
**Then** the job executes immediately with the same configuration as the scheduled version
**And** execution results are visible in monitoring dashboards
**And** the on-demand job respects the same resource limits and timeout as the scheduled version

---

## Epic 3: Secure Credential Management

Marcos can securely reference SAP and Azure credentials without knowing secret values. Juliano provisions and scopes secrets per business area with full audit trail.

### Story 3.1: Provision Key Vault with Namespace-Scoped Access

As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
I want Azure Key Vault configured with CSI driver and per-namespace managed identities,
So that secrets are isolated per business area and pods authenticate via workload identity.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** AKS cluster and namespaces are provisioned
**When** Terraform security module is applied
**Then** Key Vault `kv-hypera-dev` and `kv-hypera-prd` exist
**And** User-Assigned Managed Identity is created per namespace
**And** CSI Secret Store driver is installed on the cluster
**And** SecretProviderClass resource is deployed per namespace, scoped to that namespace's secrets only
**And** rotationPollInterval is set to 2 minutes
**And** logistics namespace pods cannot retrieve finance namespace secrets (verified by test)

### Story 3.2: Inject Secrets into CronJob Pods at Runtime

As a Business Developer (Marcos),
I want to reference secrets via environment variables that are injected automatically at runtime,
So that I never handle raw credential values in my code.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** Key Vault contains a secret for a namespace
**When** a CronJob pod starts in that namespace
**Then** the secret is mounted via CSI driver as a volume
**And** the secret value is available as an environment variable in the pod
**And** the developer's code references the secret via `os.environ["SECRET_NAME"]`
**And** no secret values appear in Kubernetes Secret objects in etcd
**And** the boilerplate template documents how to reference secrets

### Story 3.3: Enable Credential Access Auditing

As a Security Officer,
I want all credential access events logged with caller identity, timestamp, and operation type,
So that we have a complete audit trail retained for minimum 90 days.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** Key Vault diagnostic settings are configured
**When** any pod accesses a Key Vault secret
**Then** the access event is recorded with caller identity (managed identity), timestamp, and operation type
**And** audit logs are retained for minimum 90 days
**And** monthly completeness verification shows < 1% discrepancy between access count and application request count

---

## Epic 4: Automated CI/CD & Code Governance

Marcos submits code via PR, CI validates automatically, Christian reviews and approves, and ArgoCD deploys via GitOps — the complete governance pipeline.

### Story 4.1: Configure Azure DevOps Repository and PR Workflow

As a Business Developer (Marcos),
I want to push code to Azure DevOps and create Pull Requests,
So that my changes enter the governance review process.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** Azure DevOps project and repository are configured
**When** a developer pushes code and creates a PR
**Then** the PR is visible to reviewers with diff view
**And** reviewers can comment on specific code lines requesting changes
**And** branch policies require at least one approval before merge
**And** the repository structure follows the monorepo convention (`k8s/apps/<namespace>/<cronjob>/`)

### Story 4.2: Build CI Validation Pipeline

As a Code Reviewer (Christian),
I want the CI pipeline to automatically validate builds, scan for secrets, check CVEs, and verify template compliance,
So that I review only code that passes automated quality gates.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** a PR is created or updated
**When** the CI pipeline triggers automatically
**Then** Docker image builds successfully from the developer's Dockerfile
**And** secret scanning (TruffleHog/CredScan) runs and blocks PRs containing detected credentials
**And** Trivy scans the container image with zero Critical/High CVEs allowed
**And** template compliance is verified (src/ structure, structlog config, README completeness)
**And** pipeline results are visible alongside the PR
**And** total pipeline time is < 10 minutes
**And** pipeline trigger reliability is >= 99%

### Story 4.3: Enable PR Approval and Automated Image Push

As a Code Reviewer (Christian),
I want approved and merged PRs to automatically build and push Docker images to ACR,
So that approved code progresses to deployment without manual steps.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** a PR passes CI validation and is approved
**When** the reviewer merges the PR to the main branch
**Then** the CI pipeline builds the Docker image
**And** the image is tagged with Git SHA (immutable) and `latest-<env>` (mutable)
**And** the image is pushed to Azure Container Registry
**And** the image is available for ArgoCD to sync

### Story 4.4: Deploy ArgoCD with ApplicationSets for GitOps

As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
I want ArgoCD deployed with ApplicationSets that auto-discover namespaces from the Git repository,
So that deployments are declarative, self-healing, and scale automatically.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** ArgoCD is installed on the AKS cluster
**When** a new namespace directory appears in `k8s/apps/<namespace>/`
**Then** ArgoCD automatically creates an Application for that namespace
**And** ArgoCD syncs CronJob manifests from Git to the cluster within 5 minutes of merge
**And** if a CronJob manifest is manually modified in-cluster, ArgoCD reverts to Git state within 3 minutes (NFR12)
**And** manual approval gate exists for DEV to PRD promotion
**And** ArgoCD watches configured repository folders per namespace

---

## Epic 5: Monitoring, Logging & Observability

Ana can see execution status and logs from the dashboard without asking IT. Juliano has full platform-wide visibility into all namespaces, execution history, and resource consumption.

### Story 5.1: Deploy LGTM Observability Stack

As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
I want Robusta (with embedded Prometheus + Grafana), Mimir, Loki, and Tempo deployed with Azure Storage backends,
So that the monitoring infrastructure can collect metrics, logs, and traces.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** AKS cluster is operational and Azure Storage Account with 3 containers exists
**When** Terraform monitoring module is applied
**Then** Robusta is deployed with kube-prometheus-stack (Prometheus + Grafana + AlertManager)
**And** Prometheus remote-writes metrics to Mimir (Azure Blob: `mimir-blocks`)
**And** Loki is deployed collecting logs to Azure Blob (`loki-chunks`)
**And** Tempo is deployed collecting traces to Azure Blob (`tempo-traces`)
**And** Single Grafana instance connects to Mimir, Loki, and Tempo as data sources
**And** Prometheus scraping interval is <= 30 seconds for CronJob metrics
**And** OTel Operator is deployed with Python auto-instrumentation CR per namespace

### Story 5.2: Create Platform and Namespace Dashboards

As an Operations Manager (Ana),
I want Grafana dashboards showing platform overview and per-namespace drill-downs,
So that I can monitor all scripts without asking developers.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** LGTM stack is operational with metrics flowing
**When** Ana opens the Grafana dashboard
**Then** Platform Overview dashboard shows all namespaces, total jobs, and aggregate success rates
**And** Namespace View dashboard shows per-area drill-down with job status and resource usage
**And** dashboard page loads in < 3 seconds for up to 30 days of history
**And** all dashboard access requires corporate AD (SSO) authentication

### Story 5.3: Enable Execution History and Log Viewing

As an Operations Manager (Ana),
I want to view execution history, duration trends, and drill into logs for each CronJob,
So that I can troubleshoot issues independently without asking developers.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** CronJobs are running and producing metrics and logs
**When** Ana navigates to a CronJob detail dashboard
**Then** execution history shows minimum last 100 runs or 90 days with duration and status per run
**And** duration trend visualization shows performance over time
**And** clicking a specific execution links to its logs in Loki
**And** log query response time is < 5 seconds for last 7 days of logs

### Story 5.4: Collect and Display Resource Consumption Metrics

As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
I want per-CronJob CPU, memory, and duration metrics collected and displayed,
So that I can right-size resource quotas and identify resource-hungry scripts.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** Prometheus is scraping CronJob pod metrics
**When** Juliano views the resource metrics dashboard
**Then** CPU time consumed (request vs actual) is displayed per CronJob
**And** memory consumed (request vs actual) is displayed per CronJob
**And** execution duration is displayed per CronJob
**And** Loki collects logs centrally across all namespaces for all job executions

---

## Epic 6: Cost Tracking & Chargeback

Ana and Juliano can track compute costs per script and per business area, and export monthly chargeback reports for finance.

### Story 6.1: Implement Per-CronJob Cost Tracking

As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
I want compute costs (CPU-seconds, memory-seconds) calculated per CronJob execution,
So that costs are attributable to individual scripts.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** resource consumption metrics are collected per CronJob
**When** a CronJob execution completes
**Then** CPU-seconds consumed is calculated from Prometheus/Mimir metrics
**And** memory-seconds consumed is calculated from Prometheus/Mimir metrics
**And** execution duration is recorded
**And** cost data is queryable via Grafana

### Story 6.2: Aggregate and Display Namespace Cost Dashboards

As an Operations Manager (Ana),
I want cost breakdowns per CronJob and per namespace with daily/weekly/monthly aggregation,
So that I can review spending patterns and identify cost outliers.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** per-CronJob cost data is available in Mimir
**When** Ana opens the Cost Tracking dashboard
**Then** individual CronJob costs are displayed with trends
**And** namespace-level cost aggregation is available (daily, weekly, monthly)
**And** cost breakdown per CronJob within a namespace is visible

### Story 6.3: Export Monthly Chargeback Reports

As an Operations Manager (Ana),
I want to export monthly cost reports per namespace in CSV format,
So that I can submit chargeback data to finance.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** monthly cost data is aggregated per namespace
**When** Ana uses Grafana dashboard export
**Then** CSV file is generated with per-CronJob cost breakdown for the selected month
**And** namespace total is included
**And** the export includes columns: CronJob name, execution count, total CPU-seconds, total memory-seconds, total duration, estimated cost

---

## Epic 7: Alerting & Incident Response

Ana and Juliano receive proactive notifications via Teams and Email when jobs fail or behave anomalously, with severity-based routing and full alert history.

### Story 7.1: Configure Alert Routing to Teams and Email

As a Platform Admin (Juliano),
I want Robusta configured to send enriched alerts to Teams and Email based on severity levels,
So that the right people are notified of issues at the right urgency.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** Robusta is deployed with playbooks and sinks configured
**When** a job fails after all 3 retries
**Then** an enriched alert (with pod logs, resource usage, suggested fix) is sent to Teams
**And** Critical severity alerts go to Teams OPS channel + Email to on-call (immediate response)
**And** High severity alerts go to Teams OPS channel + Email (business hours response)
**And** Medium severity alerts go to Email only (next business day review)
**And** Low severity alerts appear in Grafana dashboard only (weekly review)
**And** alert is delivered within 60 seconds of the condition trigger
**And** alert is sent within 2 minutes of final retry failure
**And** mean failure detection time is < 5 minutes across all alert types (NFR10)

### Story 7.2: Implement Anomaly Detection Alerts

As an Operations Manager (Ana),
I want alerts when a job's execution time exceeds 2x its historical average,
So that performance degradation is caught before it impacts business processes.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** historical execution duration data exists for a CronJob
**When** a job execution time exceeds 2x the rolling average
**Then** a medium-severity alert is generated
**And** the alert includes the current duration, historical average, and the deviation factor
**And** the alert is routed according to severity rules (medium to Email)

### Story 7.3: Create Alert Dashboard and History

As an Operations Manager (Ana),
I want to view active alerts and full alert history in Grafana,
So that I have a complete record of incidents for governance reporting.

**Acceptance Criteria:**

**Given** alerts are being generated and routed
**When** Ana opens the Alerts dashboard
**Then** active alerts are displayed with severity, source CronJob, namespace, and timestamp
**And** alert history is searchable and filterable by date range, severity, and namespace
**And** alert severity distribution is visualized

    19→# Architecture Decision Document
    20→
    21→_This document builds collaboratively through step-by-step discovery. Sections are appended as we work through each architectural decision together._
    22→
    23→## Project Context Analysis
    24→
    25→### Requirements Overview
    26→
    27→**Functional Requirements:**
    28→47 FRs across 9 capability groups. The architecture must support two distinct planes:
    29→1. **Developer Plane** — boilerplate template, code push, PR workflow, credential references (FR1-6, FR7-13)
    30→2. **Platform Plane** — build pipeline, deployment, scheduling, monitoring, cost tracking, alerting, access control, infrastructure provisioning (FR14-47)
    31→
    32→These planes interact at well-defined boundaries: the PR merge triggers the build pipeline, the boilerplate template defines the contract between developer code and platform services, and namespace isolation separates business areas.
    33→
    34→**Non-Functional Requirements:**
    35→26 NFRs that impose specific architectural constraints:
    36→- **Security (NFR1-7):** Zero-trust credential model, namespace network isolation, RBAC verification, container vulnerability scanning, SSO-only access
    37→- **Reliability (NFR8-13):** 99.5% execution availability, auto-retry with alerting, no SPOF, ArgoCD drift self-heal, secret rotation resilience
    38→- **Scalability (NFR14-17):** 5→60+ CronJobs growth path, node autoscaling 3-10, 20 concurrent jobs per namespace
    39→- **Performance (NFR18-21):** <60s pod startup (cached), <3s dashboard load, <10min CI pipeline, <5s log queries
    40→- **Integration (NFR22-26):** SAP connectivity verification, <5min ArgoCD sync, <60s alert delivery, 99% CI trigger reliability, 30s metrics scrape interval
    41→
    42→**Scale & Complexity:**
    43→
    44→- Primary domain: Infrastructure / Platform Engineering
    45→- Complexity level: High
    46→- Estimated architectural components: 12-15 (AKS cluster, ArgoCD, CI pipelines, ACR, Key Vault + CSI driver, Robusta (Prometheus + Grafana + AlertManager + alert enrichment), LGTM backends (Mimir, Loki, Tempo), Teams alerting via Robusta sinks, namespace provisioning, boilerplate template, base Docker images, network policies, RBAC configuration)
    47→
    48→### Technical Constraints & Dependencies
    49→
    50→- **Azure-only cloud** — corporate standard, no multi-cloud consideration needed
    51→- **Kubernetes CronJobs only** — no long-running services, no event-driven execution for MVP
    52→- **Azure DevOps for source control and CI** — not GitHub, not GitLab
    53→- **ArgoCD for GitOps CD** — already decided, not evaluating alternatives
    54→- **SAP on-prem/private network** — requires VNet integration or peering from AKS to SAP endpoints
    55→- **AD group-based identity** — 4 AD groups (DEV, REVIEW, OPS, ADMIN) map to RBAC roles
    56→- **Two environments only for MVP** — DEV + PRD (QA deferred to Phase 2)
    57→- **Python 3.13** — runtime version locked
    58→- **Team size: 3 core** — Juliano (infra), Christian (K8s), Ricardo (CI/CD)
    59→
    60→### Cross-Cutting Concerns Identified
    61→
    62→1. **Secret Lifecycle** — Key Vault provisioning → CSI driver injection → namespace scoping → rotation resilience → audit logging. Touches FR21-24, NFR1-3, NFR13.
    63→2. **Namespace-as-Tenant** — provisioning → RBAC binding → resource quotas → network isolation → ArgoCD app targeting → cost attribution. Touches FR39-43, FR20, NFR5-6, NFR16, FR31-34.
    64→3. **Observability Chain** — Robusta (embedded kube-prometheus-stack) → Prometheus remote-write → Mimir (long-term metrics) + Loki (logs) + Tempo (traces) → Grafana dashboards → alert enrichment + playbooks → Teams/Email sinks → KRR cost recommendations. Touches FR25-38, NFR10, NFR19, NFR21, NFR24, NFR26.
    65→4. **Developer Contract (Boilerplate)** — template structure → Dockerfile → base image selection → src/ boundary → config → README requirements. Touches FR1-6, FR13.
    66→5. **GitOps Pipeline** — Azure DevOps PR → CI build + scan → ACR push → ArgoCD sync → CronJob update. Touches FR7-15, NFR4, NFR20, NFR23, NFR25.
    67→
    68→## Starter Template Evaluation
    69→
    70→### Primary Technology Domain
    71→
    72→Infrastructure / Platform Engineering — Terraform + Kubernetes (Helm) platform with Python CronJob workloads on Azure AKS.
    73→
    74→### Starter Options Considered
    75→
    76→This project is not a typical application with web framework starters. It is an infrastructure platform built with Terraform modules, Helm charts, and GitOps manifests. The "starter" is the project structure and tooling choices themselves.
    77→
    78→**Option 1: Monorepo Terraform + K8s Manifests (Selected)**
    79→- Single repository with `terraform/` for IaC and `k8s/` for Kubernetes manifests
    80→- Terraform modules deploy Helm charts via `helm_release` provider
    81→- ArgoCD watches `k8s/` directory for GitOps reconciliation
    82→- Aligns with existing `docs/source-tree-analysis.md` structure
    83→
    84→**Option 2: Separate Repos (Rejected)**
    85→- Terraform in one repo, K8s manifests in another
    86→- Rejected: team of 3 doesn't benefit from multi-repo overhead; single repo simplifies cross-cutting changes
    87→
    88→### Selected Approach: Monorepo with Terraform Modules + Helm Values
    89→
    90→**Rationale:** Matches existing project structure, team size, and the platform engineering domain where infrastructure and manifests are tightly coupled.
    91→
    92→### Project Structure
    93→
    94→```
    95→terraform/
    96→├── modules/
    97→│   ├── aks/                      # AKS cluster provisioning
    98→│   ├── monitoring/               # LGTM stack + Robusta deployment via Helm provider
    99→│   │   ├── main.tf              # helm_release for Robusta, Mimir, Loki, Tempo
   100→│   │   ├── storage.tf           # Azure Storage Account + 3 containers
   101→│   │   ├── identity.tf          # User-Assigned Managed Identity (workload identity)
   102→│   │   ├── variables.tf
   103→│   │   └── outputs.tf
   104→│   ├── argocd/                   # ArgoCD deployment + ApplicationSets
   105→│   ├── namespaces/               # Namespace provisioning + RBAC + quotas
   106→│   ├── networking/               # VNet, peering, network policies
   107→│   ├── security/                 # Key Vault, CSI driver, secret classes
   108→│   └── ci/                       # ACR, service connections, build pipelines
   109→
   110→k8s/
   111→├── platform/
   112→│   ├── monitoring/
   113→│   │   ├── robusta-values.yaml   # Robusta Helm values (Prometheus remote-write to Mimir)
   114→│   │   ├── mimir-values.yaml     # Mimir distributed values (Azure Blob: mimir-blocks)
   115→│   │   ├── loki-values.yaml      # Loki values (Azure Blob: loki-chunks)
   116→│   │   ├── tempo-values.yaml     # Tempo distributed values (Azure Blob: tempo-traces)
   117→│   │   └── dashboards/           # Custom Grafana dashboard JSONs
   118→│   ├── argocd/
   119→│   └── base-configs/
   120→├── apps/                          # ArgoCD Application manifests per namespace
   121→└── boilerplate/                   # CronJob template for developers
   122→```
   123→
   124→### Version Table
   125→
   126→| Component | Version | Source |
   127→|-----------|---------|--------|
   128→| Terraform | >= 1.9 | hashicorp.com |
   129→| AzureRM Provider | ~> 4.x | registry.terraform.io |
   130→| Helm Provider | ~> 2.x | registry.terraform.io |
   131→| Robusta | 0.10.30+ | robusta-charts.storage.googleapis.com |
   132→| Mimir (distributed) | latest | grafana.github.io/helm-charts |
   133→| Loki | 6.53.0 | grafana.github.io/helm-charts |
   134→| Tempo (distributed) | latest | grafana.github.io/helm-charts |
   135→| ArgoCD | latest stable | argoproj.github.io/argo-helm |
   136→| Python | 3.13 | base Docker images |
   137→
   138→### Azure Storage for LGTM Backends
   139→
   140→The `terraform/modules/monitoring/storage.tf` provisions:
   141→
   142→| Resource | Purpose |
   143→|----------|---------|
   144→| Storage Account (`stlgtm<env>`) | Shared account for all LGTM backends |
   145→| Container: `mimir-blocks` | Mimir TSDB blocks (long-term metrics) |
   146→| Container: `loki-chunks` | Loki log chunks + index |
   147→| Container: `tempo-traces` | Tempo trace data |
   148→| User-Assigned Managed Identity | Workload identity for pod → storage auth |
   149→
   150→### PRD Requirement Mapping
   151→
   152→| Requirement | How Architecture Satisfies |
   153→|-------------|---------------------------|
   154→| FR1-6 (Boilerplate) | `k8s/boilerplate/` template with Dockerfile, src/ boundary, config |
   155→| FR7-13 (CI/CD) | Azure DevOps pipelines → ACR → ArgoCD sync |
   156→| FR14-24 (Platform) | Terraform modules for AKS, namespaces, Key Vault, networking |
   157→| FR25-30 (Monitoring) | Robusta + Mimir (365d long-term metrics retention) |
   158→| FR31-34 (Cost Tracking) | Mimir historical metrics + KRR cost recommendations |
   159→| FR35-38 (Alerting) | Robusta alert enrichment → Teams/Email sinks |
   160→| FR39-47 (Access/Infra) | Terraform namespace module with RBAC, quotas, network policies |
   161→| NFR19 (<3s dashboard) | Mimir query frontend + caching |
   162→| NFR21 (<5s log queries) | Loki TSDB schema v13 + index gateway |
   163→| New: Distributed Tracing | Tempo enables CronJob execution tracing via OpenTelemetry |
   164→
   165→### Data Flow — Observability
   166→
   167→```
   168→CronJob pods
   169→  ├── metrics → Prometheus (Robusta) → remote-write → Mimir (Azure Blob)
   170→  ├── logs → Loki (Azure Blob)
   171→  └── traces → Tempo (Azure Blob) [via OpenTelemetry]
   172→                    ↓
   173→              Grafana (Robusta embedded)
   174→              connects to all 3 backends
   175→                    ↓
   176→              Robusta alert enrichment → Teams/Email sinks
   177→```
   178→
   179→**Note:** Single Grafana instance (embedded in Robusta's kube-prometheus-stack) connects to Mimir, Loki, and Tempo as data sources. No duplicate Grafana deployment.
   180→
   181→## Core Architectural Decisions
   182→
   183→### Decision Priority Analysis
   184→
   185→**Critical Decisions (Block Implementation):**
   186→1. Terraform state backend and segmentation
   187→2. Secret management pattern (Key Vault → CSI Driver)
   188→3. SAP connectivity pattern
   189→4. ArgoCD application management model
   190→5. Network policy model
   191→
   192→**Important Decisions (Shape Architecture):**
   193→6. Container vulnerability scanning
   194→7. Image tagging and promotion strategy
   195→8. OpenTelemetry instrumentation approach
   196→9. Log format standardization
   197→10. Alert routing rules
   198→
   199→**Deferred Decisions (Post-MVP):**
   200→- Multi-cluster federation (Phase 2 with QA environment)
   201→- Service mesh consideration (not needed for CronJobs)
   202→- Advanced cost showback per-namespace (Phase 2)
   203→
   204→### Category 1: State & Storage
   205→
   206→**Decision 1.1: Terraform State Backend**
   207→- **Choice:** Azure Storage Account with blob backend
   208→- **Version:** AzureRM Provider ~> 4.60
   209→- **Rationale:** Azure-only constraint makes Azure Storage the natural choice. No Terraform Cloud dependency, keeps everything within corporate Azure tenant. State encryption at rest via Azure Storage SSE.
   210→- **Configuration:**
   211→  - Storage Account: `sttfstate<env><region>` with RA-GRS replication
   212→  - Container: `tfstate`
   213→  - State locking: Azure Blob lease (built-in with azurerm backend)
   214→  - Access: Managed Identity for CI/CD service connections
   215→
   216→**Decision 1.2: State File Segmentation**
   217→- **Choice:** Per-module state files with shared networking/identity state
   218→- **Rationale:** Team of 3 needs blast radius isolation. A failed monitoring deploy must not block namespace provisioning. Per-module state allows parallel `terraform apply` across modules.
   219→- **State Keys:**
   220→  - `networking/terraform.tfstate` — VNet, subnets, peering
   221→  - `aks/terraform.tfstate` — AKS cluster, node pools
   222→  - `security/terraform.tfstate` — Key Vault, CSI driver, managed identities
   223→  - `monitoring/terraform.tfstate` — LGTM stack, Robusta, storage
   224→  - `argocd/terraform.tfstate` — ArgoCD deployment
   225→  - `namespaces/terraform.tfstate` — Namespace provisioning, RBAC, quotas
   226→  - `ci/terraform.tfstate` — ACR, build pipelines, service connections
   227→
   228→### Category 2: Secret Management & Zero-Trust
   229→
   230→**Decision 2.1: Key Vault → CSI Driver Pattern**
   231→- **Choice:** One Key Vault per environment, SecretProviderClass per namespace
   232→- **Rationale:** Single Key Vault simplifies management for team of 3. Namespace-scoped SecretProviderClass resources ensure tenant isolation. CSI driver mounts secrets as volumes — no Kubernetes Secret objects stored in etcd.
   233→- **Pattern:**
   234→  - Key Vault: `kv-hypera-<env>` (DEV, PRD)
   235→  - Access: User-Assigned Managed Identity per namespace (workload identity)
   236→  - SecretProviderClass: one per namespace, scoped to that namespace's secrets
   237→  - Sync frequency: `rotationPollInterval: 2m` (CSI driver default)
   238→
   239→**Decision 2.2: Network Policy Model**
   240→- **Choice:** Default-deny per namespace with explicit allowlists
   241→- **Rationale:** NFR5 requires namespace network isolation. Default-deny ensures new namespaces are isolated by default. Explicit NetworkPolicy resources allow only required egress (SAP endpoints, Azure services, DNS).
   242→- **Implementation:** Calico or Azure NPM (Azure CNI native). Terraform namespace module provisions default-deny + standard egress rules.
   243→
   244→**Decision 2.3: Container Vulnerability Scanning**
   245→- **Choice:** Trivy Operator (continuous in-cluster scanning)
   246→- **Version:** Trivy v0.69.x / Trivy Operator latest
   247→- **Rationale:** Open-source, Kubernetes-native, scans images + IaC + RBAC. Results as CRDs queryable via kubectl. CI pipeline also runs `trivy image` pre-push to ACR. Complements (not replaces) Microsoft Defender for Containers if corporate mandates it.
   248→- **Affects:** CI pipeline definition, ACR push gate, continuous cluster scanning
   249→
   250→### Category 3: Service Connectivity
   251→
   252→**Decision 3.1: SAP Connectivity Pattern**
   253→- **Choice:** VNet peering from AKS VNet to SAP private network
   254→- **Rationale:** FR22 requires SAP on-prem/private connectivity. VNet peering provides lowest latency, no data transit costs within region, and uses existing corporate network topology. Private DNS zones resolve SAP hostnames within the peered network.
   255→- **Implementation:**
   256→  - Terraform `azurerm_virtual_network_peering` in networking module
   257→  - Private DNS zone for SAP endpoint resolution
   258→  - Network policies allow egress to SAP CIDR only from authorized namespaces
   259→
   260→**Decision 3.2: CronJob → External Service Auth**
   261→- **Choice:** Workload Identity (User-Assigned Managed Identity per namespace)
   262→- **Rationale:** Zero-trust model — no static credentials. Each namespace's managed identity is scoped to only the Azure resources and SAP endpoints that namespace's CronJobs need. Key Vault stores any non-Azure credentials (SAP RFC passwords) injected via CSI driver.
   263→- **Pattern:** Pod → Workload Identity → Azure AD token → Azure resource / Key Vault → SAP credential
   264→
   265→**Decision 3.3: DNS Resolution**
   266→- **Choice:** Azure Private DNS Zones linked to AKS VNet
   267→- **Rationale:** CronJobs need to resolve SAP hostnames, Key Vault endpoints, and Storage Account endpoints without public DNS exposure. CoreDNS in AKS forwards to Azure DNS which resolves private zones.
   268→
   269→### Category 4: GitOps & Pipeline
   270→
   271→**Decision 4.1: ArgoCD Application Management**
   272→- **Choice:** ApplicationSets with Git generator per namespace
   273→- **Version:** ArgoCD Helm chart v9.4.x
   274→- **Rationale:** ApplicationSets auto-generate ArgoCD Applications from directory structure. When a new namespace directory appears in `k8s/apps/<namespace>/`, ArgoCD automatically creates the Application. Scales from 5 to 60+ CronJobs without manual Application creation.
   275→- **Pattern:**
   276→  ```yaml
   277→  # ApplicationSet watches k8s/apps/*/
   278→  generators:
   279→    - git:
   280→        repoURL: <azure-devops-repo>
   281→        directories:
   282→          - path: k8s/apps/*
   283→  ```
   284→
   285→**Decision 4.2: Image Tagging Strategy**
   286→- **Choice:** Git SHA tags for traceability + `latest-<env>` mutable tags for ArgoCD
   287→- **Rationale:** Git SHA provides immutable audit trail (NFR1). Mutable `latest-dev` / `latest-prd` tags let ArgoCD Image Updater auto-sync. CI pipeline tags both: `acr.azurecr.io/app:abc1234` + `acr.azurecr.io/app:latest-dev`.
   288→- **Affects:** CI pipeline, ArgoCD Image Updater config, ACR retention policy
   289→
   290→**Decision 4.3: Promotion Model DEV → PRD**
   291→- **Choice:** Manual approval gate via Azure DevOps Release Pipeline
   292→- **Rationale:** Team of 3, high-consequence workloads (SAP integrations). Automated promotion is premature for MVP. PR to `prd` branch triggers pipeline with manual approval stage. ArgoCD syncs PRD cluster from `prd` branch.
   293→- **Flow:** Dev branch merge → CI builds → DEV auto-deploy → Manual approval → PRD branch merge → PRD auto-deploy
   294→
   295→**Decision 4.4: Base Docker Image Management**
   296→- **Choice:** Shared base images in ACR, maintained by infra team
   297→- **Rationale:** Python 3.13 locked. Single `base-python:3.13-slim` image with common dependencies (Azure SDK, SAP libraries). Developers' Dockerfiles `FROM` this base. Trivy scans base image on schedule. Reduces per-CronJob image build time and ensures consistent security baseline.
   298→
   299→### Category 5: Observability Decisions
   300→
   301→**Decision 5.1: OpenTelemetry Instrumentation**
   302→- **Choice:** OTel Operator auto-instrumentation for Python
   303→- **Version:** OpenTelemetry Operator latest, autoinstrumentation-python 0.39b0+
   304→- **Rationale:** Zero-code instrumentation via pod annotations. OTel Operator injects Python auto-instrumentation at pod startup. No developer code changes required. Traces sent to Tempo via OTel Collector (HTTP port 4318).
   305→- **Pattern:**
   306→  ```yaml
   307→  # Instrumentation CR in each namespace
   308→  apiVersion: opentelemetry.io/v1alpha1
   309→  kind: Instrumentation
   310→  metadata:
   311→    name: python-instrumentation
   312→  spec:
   313→    exporter:
   314→      endpoint: http://tempo-distributor.monitoring:4318
   315→    python:
   316→      image: ghcr.io/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-operator/autoinstrumentation-python:latest
   317→  ```
   318→- **Pod annotation:** `instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-python: "true"`
   319→
   320→**Decision 5.2: Log Format Standardization**
   321→- **Choice:** Structured JSON logs with standard fields
   322→- **Rationale:** NFR21 requires <5s log queries. Structured JSON enables Loki to parse and index fields without regex. Boilerplate template includes Python `structlog` configuration with standard fields: `timestamp`, `level`, `message`, `cronjob_name`, `namespace`, `trace_id`.
   323→- **Standard Fields:**
   324→  - `timestamp` (ISO 8601)
   325→  - `level` (INFO, WARNING, ERROR)
   326→  - `message`
   327→  - `cronjob_name`
   328→  - `namespace`
   329→  - `trace_id` (correlated with Tempo)
   330→  - `run_id` (unique per CronJob execution)
   331→
   332→**Decision 5.3: Alert Routing Rules**
   333→- **Choice:** Severity-based routing via Robusta sinks
   334→- **Rationale:** NFR24 requires <60s alert delivery. Robusta playbooks enrich alerts with pod logs, resource usage, and suggested fixes. Routing by severity ensures the right people get the right alerts.
   335→- **Routing:**
   336→  | Severity | Channel | Response |
   337→  |----------|---------|----------|
   338→  | Critical | Teams (OPS channel) + Email to on-call | Immediate |
   339→  | Warning | Teams (OPS channel) | Business hours |
   340→  | Info | Grafana dashboard only | Review weekly |
   341→- **Affects:** Robusta playbooks, Teams webhook configuration, email sink
   342→
   343→### Decision Impact Analysis
   344→
   345→**Implementation Sequence:**
   346→1. Networking (VNet, peering, DNS) — foundation for everything
   347→2. AKS cluster — requires networking
   348→3. Security (Key Vault, CSI driver, managed identities) — requires AKS
   349→4. Monitoring (LGTM + Robusta + OTel Operator) — requires AKS + storage
   350→5. ArgoCD — requires AKS + networking
   351→6. Namespaces (RBAC, quotas, network policies, SecretProviderClass) — requires all above
   352→7. CI (ACR, pipelines, base images) — requires AKS + ACR
   353→8. Boilerplate template — requires all platform services running
   354→
   355→**Cross-Component Dependencies:**
   356→- Workload Identity → used by LGTM pods (storage access), CronJob pods (Key Vault, SAP), ArgoCD (repo access)
   357→- VNet peering → required by SAP connectivity, Private DNS zones, Key Vault private endpoints
   358→- Trivy → scans base images (ACR), cluster resources (AKS), and Helm charts (ArgoCD)
   359→- OTel Operator → depends on Tempo being deployed first (Instrumentation CR references Tempo endpoint)
   360→- Structured logging → boilerplate template must include structlog config that correlates with Tempo trace_id
   361→
   362→### Updated Version Table
   363→
   364→| Component | Version | Source |
   365→|-----------|---------|--------|
   366→| Terraform | >= 1.14 | [hashicorp.com](https://releases.hashicorp.com/terraform/) |
   367→| AzureRM Provider | ~> 4.60 | [registry.terraform.io](https://registry.terraform.io/providers/hashicorp/azurerm/latest) |
   368→| Helm Provider | ~> 2.x | registry.terraform.io |
   369→| Robusta | 0.10.30+ | robusta-charts.storage.googleapis.com |
   370→| Mimir (distributed) | latest (v4.1+) | [grafana.github.io/helm-charts](https://grafana.com/docs/helm-charts/mimir-distributed/latest/) |
   371→| Loki | 6.53.0 | grafana.github.io/helm-charts |
   372→| Tempo (distributed) | 1.61.x | [grafana.github.io/helm-charts](https://artifacthub.io/packages/helm/grafana/tempo-distributed) |
   373→| ArgoCD | Helm chart v9.4.x | [argoproj.github.io/argo-helm](https://artifacthub.io/packages/helm/argo/argo-cd) |
   374→| Trivy Operator | v0.69.x | [aquasecurity/trivy](https://github.com/aquasecurity/trivy) |
   375→| OTel Operator | latest | [open-telemetry/opentelemetry-operator](https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-operator) |
   376→| Python | 3.13 | base Docker images |
   377→

# Architecture Decision Document

_This document builds collaboratively through step-by-step discovery. Sections are appended as we work through each architectural decision together._

## Project Context Analysis

### Requirements Overview

**Functional Requirements:**
47 FRs across 9 capability groups. The architecture must support two distinct planes:
1. **Developer Plane** — boilerplate template, code push, PR workflow, credential references (FR1-6, FR7-13)
2. **Platform Plane** — build pipeline, deployment, scheduling, monitoring, cost tracking, alerting, access control, infrastructure provisioning (FR14-47)

These planes interact at well-defined boundaries: the PR merge triggers the build pipeline, the boilerplate template defines the contract between developer code and platform services, and namespace isolation separates business areas.

**Non-Functional Requirements:**
26 NFRs that impose specific architectural constraints:
- **Security (NFR1-7):** Zero-trust credential model, namespace network isolation, RBAC verification, container vulnerability scanning, SSO-only access
- **Reliability (NFR8-13):** 99.5% execution availability, auto-retry with alerting, no SPOF, ArgoCD drift self-heal, secret rotation resilience
- **Scalability (NFR14-17):** 5→60+ CronJobs growth path, node autoscaling 3-10, 20 concurrent jobs per namespace
- **Performance (NFR18-21):** <60s pod startup (cached), <3s dashboard load, <10min CI pipeline, <5s log queries
- **Integration (NFR22-26):** SAP connectivity verification, <5min ArgoCD sync, <60s alert delivery, 99% CI trigger reliability, 30s metrics scrape interval

**Scale & Complexity:**

- Primary domain: Infrastructure / Platform Engineering
- Complexity level: High
- Estimated architectural components: 12-15 (AKS cluster, ArgoCD, CI pipelines, ACR, Key Vault + CSI driver, Robusta (Prometheus + Grafana + AlertManager + alert enrichment), LGTM backends (Mimir, Loki, Tempo), Teams alerting via Robusta sinks, namespace provisioning, boilerplate template, base Docker images, network policies, RBAC configuration)

### Technical Constraints & Dependencies

- **Azure-only cloud** — corporate standard, no multi-cloud consideration needed
- **Kubernetes CronJobs only** — no long-running services, no event-driven execution for MVP
- **Azure DevOps for source control and CI** — not GitHub, not GitLab
- **ArgoCD for GitOps CD** — already decided, not evaluating alternatives
- **SAP on-prem/private network** — requires VNet integration or peering from AKS to SAP endpoints
- **AD group-based identity** — 4 AD groups (DEV, REVIEW, OPS, ADMIN) map to RBAC roles
- **Two environments only for MVP** — DEV + PRD (QA deferred to Phase 2)
- **Python 3.13** — runtime version locked
- **Team size: 3 core** — Juliano (infra), Christian (K8s), Ricardo (CI/CD)

### Cross-Cutting Concerns Identified

1. **Secret Lifecycle** — Key Vault provisioning → CSI driver injection → namespace scoping → rotation resilience → audit logging. Touches FR21-24, NFR1-3, NFR13.
2. **Namespace-as-Tenant** — provisioning → RBAC binding → resource quotas → network isolation → ArgoCD app targeting → cost attribution. Touches FR39-43, FR20, NFR5-6, NFR16, FR31-34.
3. **Observability Chain** — Robusta (embedded kube-prometheus-stack) → Prometheus remote-write → Mimir (long-term metrics) + Loki (logs) + Tempo (traces) → Grafana dashboards → alert enrichment + playbooks → Teams/Email sinks → KRR cost recommendations. Touches FR25-38, NFR10, NFR19, NFR21, NFR24, NFR26.
4. **Developer Contract (Boilerplate)** — template structure → Dockerfile → base image selection → src/ boundary → config → README requirements. Touches FR1-6, FR13.
5. **GitOps Pipeline** — Azure DevOps PR → CI build + scan → ACR push → ArgoCD sync → CronJob update. Touches FR7-15, NFR4, NFR20, NFR23, NFR25.

## Starter Template Evaluation

### Primary Technology Domain

Infrastructure / Platform Engineering — Terraform + Kubernetes (Helm) platform with Python CronJob workloads on Azure AKS.

### Starter Options Considered

This project is not a typical application with web framework starters. It is an infrastructure platform built with Terraform modules, Helm charts, and GitOps manifests. The "starter" is the project structure and tooling choices themselves.

**Option 1: Monorepo Terraform + K8s Manifests (Selected)**
- Single repository with `terraform/` for IaC and `k8s/` for Kubernetes manifests
- Terraform modules deploy Helm charts via `helm_release` provider
- ArgoCD watches `k8s/` directory for GitOps reconciliation
- Aligns with existing `docs/source-tree-analysis.md` structure

**Option 2: Separate Repos (Rejected)**
- Terraform in one repo, K8s manifests in another
- Rejected: team of 3 doesn't benefit from multi-repo overhead; single repo simplifies cross-cutting changes

### Selected Approach: Monorepo with Terraform Modules + Helm Values

**Rationale:** Matches existing project structure, team size, and the platform engineering domain where infrastructure and manifests are tightly coupled.

### Project Structure

```
terraform/
├── modules/
│   ├── aks/                      # AKS cluster provisioning
│   ├── monitoring/               # LGTM stack + Robusta deployment via Helm provider
│   │   ├── main.tf              # helm_release for Robusta, Mimir, Loki, Tempo
│   │   ├── storage.tf           # Azure Storage Account + 3 containers
│   │   ├── identity.tf          # User-Assigned Managed Identity (workload identity)
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   └── outputs.tf
│   ├── argocd/                   # ArgoCD deployment + ApplicationSets
│   ├── namespaces/               # Namespace provisioning + RBAC + quotas
│   ├── networking/               # VNet, peering, network policies
│   ├── security/                 # Key Vault, CSI driver, secret classes
│   └── ci/                       # ACR, service connections, build pipelines

k8s/
├── platform/
│   ├── monitoring/
│   │   ├── robusta-values.yaml   # Robusta Helm values (Prometheus remote-write to Mimir)
│   │   ├── mimir-values.yaml     # Mimir distributed values (Azure Blob: mimir-blocks)
│   │   ├── loki-values.yaml      # Loki values (Azure Blob: loki-chunks)
│   │   ├── tempo-values.yaml     # Tempo distributed values (Azure Blob: tempo-traces)
│   │   └── dashboards/           # Custom Grafana dashboard JSONs
│   ├── argocd/
│   └── base-configs/
├── apps/                          # ArgoCD Application manifests per namespace
└── boilerplate/                   # CronJob template for developers
```

### Version Table

| Component | Version | Source |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| Terraform | >= 1.9 | hashicorp.com |
| AzureRM Provider | ~> 4.x | registry.terraform.io |
| Helm Provider | ~> 2.x | registry.terraform.io |
| Robusta | 0.10.30+ | robusta-charts.storage.googleapis.com |
| Mimir (distributed) | latest | grafana.github.io/helm-charts |
| Loki | 6.53.0 | grafana.github.io/helm-charts |
| Tempo (distributed) | latest | grafana.github.io/helm-charts |
| ArgoCD | latest stable | argoproj.github.io/argo-helm |
| Python | 3.13 | base Docker images |

### Azure Storage for LGTM Backends

The `terraform/modules/monitoring/storage.tf` provisions:

| Resource | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| Storage Account (`stlgtm<env>`) | Shared account for all LGTM backends |
| Container: `mimir-blocks` | Mimir TSDB blocks (long-term metrics) |
| Container: `loki-chunks` | Loki log chunks + index |
| Container: `tempo-traces` | Tempo trace data |
| User-Assigned Managed Identity | Workload identity for pod → storage auth |

### PRD Requirement Mapping

| Requirement | How Architecture Satisfies |
|-------------|---------------------------|
| FR1-6 (Boilerplate) | `k8s/boilerplate/` template with Dockerfile, src/ boundary, config |
| FR7-13 (CI/CD) | Azure DevOps pipelines → ACR → ArgoCD sync |
| FR14-24 (Platform) | Terraform modules for AKS, namespaces, Key Vault, networking |
| FR25-30 (Monitoring) | Robusta + Mimir (365d long-term metrics retention) |
| FR31-34 (Cost Tracking) | Mimir historical metrics + KRR cost recommendations |
| FR35-38 (Alerting) | Robusta alert enrichment → Teams/Email sinks |
| FR39-47 (Access/Infra) | Terraform namespace module with RBAC, quotas, network policies |
| NFR19 (<3s dashboard) | Mimir query frontend + caching |
| NFR21 (<5s log queries) | Loki TSDB schema v13 + index gateway |
| New: Distributed Tracing | Tempo enables CronJob execution tracing via OpenTelemetry |

### Data Flow — Observability

```
CronJob pods
  ├── metrics → Prometheus (Robusta) → remote-write → Mimir (Azure Blob)
  ├── logs → Loki (Azure Blob)
  └── traces → Tempo (Azure Blob) [via OpenTelemetry]
                    ↓
              Grafana (Robusta embedded)
              connects to all 3 backends
                    ↓
              Robusta alert enrichment → Teams/Email sinks
```

**Note:** Single Grafana instance (embedded in Robusta's kube-prometheus-stack) connects to Mimir, Loki, and Tempo as data sources. No duplicate Grafana deployment.

## Core Architectural Decisions

### Decision Priority Analysis

**Critical Decisions (Block Implementation):**
1. Terraform state backend and segmentation
2. Secret management pattern (Key Vault → CSI Driver)
3. SAP connectivity pattern
4. ArgoCD application management model
5. Network policy model

**Important Decisions (Shape Architecture):**
6. Container vulnerability scanning
7. Image tagging and promotion strategy
8. OpenTelemetry instrumentation approach
9. Log format standardization
10. Alert routing rules

**Deferred Decisions (Post-MVP):**
- Multi-cluster federation (Phase 2 with QA environment)
- Service mesh consideration (not needed for CronJobs)
- Advanced cost showback per-namespace (Phase 2)

### Category 1: State & Storage

**Decision 1.1: Terraform State Backend**
- **Choice:** Azure Storage Account with blob backend
- **Version:** AzureRM Provider ~> 4.60
- **Rationale:** Azure-only constraint makes Azure Storage the natural choice. No Terraform Cloud dependency, keeps everything within corporate Azure tenant. State encryption at rest via Azure Storage SSE.
- **Configuration:**
  - Storage Account: `sttfstate<env><region>` with RA-GRS replication
  - Container: `tfstate`
  - State locking: Azure Blob lease (built-in with azurerm backend)
  - Access: Managed Identity for CI/CD service connections

**Decision 1.2: State File Segmentation**
- **Choice:** Per-module state files with shared networking/identity state
- **Rationale:** Team of 3 needs blast radius isolation. A failed monitoring deploy must not block namespace provisioning. Per-module state allows parallel `terraform apply` across modules.
- **State Keys:**
  - `networking/terraform.tfstate` — VNet, subnets, peering
  - `aks/terraform.tfstate` — AKS cluster, node pools
  - `security/terraform.tfstate` — Key Vault, CSI driver, managed identities
  - `monitoring/terraform.tfstate` — LGTM stack, Robusta, storage
  - `argocd/terraform.tfstate` — ArgoCD deployment
  - `namespaces/terraform.tfstate` — Namespace provisioning, RBAC, quotas
  - `ci/terraform.tfstate` — ACR, build pipelines, service connections

### Category 2: Secret Management & Zero-Trust

**Decision 2.1: Key Vault → CSI Driver Pattern**
- **Choice:** One Key Vault per environment, SecretProviderClass per namespace
- **Rationale:** Single Key Vault simplifies management for team of 3. Namespace-scoped SecretProviderClass resources ensure tenant isolation. CSI driver mounts secrets as volumes — no Kubernetes Secret objects stored in etcd.
- **Pattern:**
  - Key Vault: `kv-hypera-<env>` (DEV, PRD)
  - Access: User-Assigned Managed Identity per namespace (workload identity)
  - SecretProviderClass: one per namespace, scoped to that namespace's secrets
  - Sync frequency: `rotationPollInterval: 2m` (CSI driver default)

**Decision 2.2: Network Policy Model**
- **Choice:** Default-deny per namespace with explicit allowlists
- **Rationale:** NFR5 requires namespace network isolation. Default-deny ensures new namespaces are isolated by default. Explicit NetworkPolicy resources allow only required egress (SAP endpoints, Azure services, DNS).
- **Implementation:** Calico or Azure NPM (Azure CNI native). Terraform namespace module provisions default-deny + standard egress rules.

**Decision 2.3: Container Vulnerability Scanning**
- **Choice:** Trivy Operator (continuous in-cluster scanning)
- **Version:** Trivy v0.69.x / Trivy Operator latest
- **Rationale:** Open-source, Kubernetes-native, scans images + IaC + RBAC. Results as CRDs queryable via kubectl. CI pipeline also runs `trivy image` pre-push to ACR. Complements (not replaces) Microsoft Defender for Containers if corporate mandates it.
- **Affects:** CI pipeline definition, ACR push gate, continuous cluster scanning

### Category 3: Service Connectivity

**Decision 3.1: SAP Connectivity Pattern**
- **Choice:** VNet peering from AKS VNet to SAP private network
- **Rationale:** FR22 requires SAP on-prem/private connectivity. VNet peering provides lowest latency, no data transit costs within region, and uses existing corporate network topology. Private DNS zones resolve SAP hostnames within the peered network.
- **Implementation:**
  - Terraform `azurerm_virtual_network_peering` in networking module
  - Private DNS zone for SAP endpoint resolution
  - Network policies allow egress to SAP CIDR only from authorized namespaces

**Decision 3.2: CronJob → External Service Auth**
- **Choice:** Workload Identity (User-Assigned Managed Identity per namespace)
- **Rationale:** Zero-trust model — no static credentials. Each namespace's managed identity is scoped to only the Azure resources and SAP endpoints that namespace's CronJobs need. Key Vault stores any non-Azure credentials (SAP RFC passwords) injected via CSI driver.
- **Pattern:** Pod → Workload Identity → Azure AD token → Azure resource / Key Vault → SAP credential

**Decision 3.3: DNS Resolution**
- **Choice:** Azure Private DNS Zones linked to AKS VNet
- **Rationale:** CronJobs need to resolve SAP hostnames, Key Vault endpoints, and Storage Account endpoints without public DNS exposure. CoreDNS in AKS forwards to Azure DNS which resolves private zones.

### Category 4: GitOps & Pipeline

**Decision 4.1: ArgoCD Application Management**
- **Choice:** ApplicationSets with Git generator per namespace
- **Version:** ArgoCD Helm chart v9.4.x
- **Rationale:** ApplicationSets auto-generate ArgoCD Applications from directory structure. When a new namespace directory appears in `k8s/apps/<namespace>/`, ArgoCD automatically creates the Application. Scales from 5 to 60+ CronJobs without manual Application creation.
- **Pattern:**
  ```yaml
  # ApplicationSet watches k8s/apps/*/
  generators:
    - git:
        repoURL: <azure-devops-repo>
        directories:
          - path: k8s/apps/*
  ```

**Decision 4.2: Image Tagging Strategy**
- **Choice:** Git SHA tags for traceability + `latest-<env>` mutable tags for ArgoCD
- **Rationale:** Git SHA provides immutable audit trail (NFR1). Mutable `latest-dev` / `latest-prd` tags let ArgoCD Image Updater auto-sync. CI pipeline tags both: `acr.azurecr.io/app:abc1234` + `acr.azurecr.io/app:latest-dev`.
- **Affects:** CI pipeline, ArgoCD Image Updater config, ACR retention policy

**Decision 4.3: Promotion Model DEV → PRD**
- **Choice:** Manual approval gate via Azure DevOps Release Pipeline
- **Rationale:** Team of 3, high-consequence workloads (SAP integrations). Automated promotion is premature for MVP. PR to `prd` branch triggers pipeline with manual approval stage. ArgoCD syncs PRD cluster from `prd` branch.
- **Flow:** Dev branch merge → CI builds → DEV auto-deploy → Manual approval → PRD branch merge → PRD auto-deploy

**Decision 4.4: Base Docker Image Management**
- **Choice:** Shared base images in ACR, maintained by infra team
- **Rationale:** Python 3.13 locked. Single `base-python:3.13-slim` image with common dependencies (Azure SDK, SAP libraries). Developers' Dockerfiles `FROM` this base. Trivy scans base image on schedule. Reduces per-CronJob image build time and ensures consistent security baseline.

### Category 5: Observability Decisions

**Decision 5.1: OpenTelemetry Instrumentation**
- **Choice:** OTel Operator auto-instrumentation for Python
- **Version:** OpenTelemetry Operator latest, autoinstrumentation-python 0.39b0+
- **Rationale:** Zero-code instrumentation via pod annotations. OTel Operator injects Python auto-instrumentation at pod startup. No developer code changes required. Traces sent to Tempo via OTel Collector (HTTP port 4318).
- **Pattern:**
  ```yaml
  # Instrumentation CR in each namespace
  apiVersion: opentelemetry.io/v1alpha1
  kind: Instrumentation
  metadata:
    name: python-instrumentation
  spec:
    exporter:
      endpoint: http://tempo-distributor.monitoring:4318
    python:
      image: ghcr.io/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-operator/autoinstrumentation-python:latest
  ```
- **Pod annotation:** `instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-python: "true"`

**Decision 5.2: Log Format Standardization**
- **Choice:** Structured JSON logs with standard fields
- **Rationale:** NFR21 requires <5s log queries. Structured JSON enables Loki to parse and index fields without regex. Boilerplate template includes Python `structlog` configuration with standard fields: `timestamp`, `level`, `message`, `cronjob_name`, `namespace`, `trace_id`.
- **Standard Fields:**
  - `timestamp` (ISO 8601)
  - `level` (INFO, WARNING, ERROR)
  - `message`
  - `cronjob_name`
  - `namespace`
  - `trace_id` (correlated with Tempo)
  - `run_id` (unique per CronJob execution)

**Decision 5.3: Alert Routing Rules**
- **Choice:** Severity-based routing via Robusta sinks
- **Rationale:** NFR24 requires <60s alert delivery. Robusta playbooks enrich alerts with pod logs, resource usage, and suggested fixes. Routing by severity ensures the right people get the right alerts.
- **Routing:**
  | Severity | Channel | Response |
  |----------|---------|----------|
  | Critical | Teams (OPS channel) + Email to on-call | Immediate |
  | Warning | Teams (OPS channel) | Business hours |
  | Info | Grafana dashboard only | Review weekly |
- **Affects:** Robusta playbooks, Teams webhook configuration, email sink

### Decision Impact Analysis

**Implementation Sequence:**
1. Networking (VNet, peering, DNS) — foundation for everything
2. AKS cluster — requires networking
3. Security (Key Vault, CSI driver, managed identities) — requires AKS
4. Monitoring (LGTM + Robusta + OTel Operator) — requires AKS + storage
5. ArgoCD — requires AKS + networking
6. Namespaces (RBAC, quotas, network policies, SecretProviderClass) — requires all above
7. CI (ACR, pipelines, base images) — requires AKS + ACR
8. Boilerplate template — requires all platform services running

**Cross-Component Dependencies:**
- Workload Identity → used by LGTM pods (storage access), CronJob pods (Key Vault, SAP), ArgoCD (repo access)
- VNet peering → required by SAP connectivity, Private DNS zones, Key Vault private endpoints
- Trivy → scans base images (ACR), cluster resources (AKS), and Helm charts (ArgoCD)
- OTel Operator → depends on Tempo being deployed first (Instrumentation CR references Tempo endpoint)
- Structured logging → boilerplate template must include structlog config that correlates with Tempo trace_id

### Updated Version Table

| Component | Version | Source |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| Terraform | >= 1.14 | [hashicorp.com](https://releases.hashicorp.com/terraform/) |
| AzureRM Provider | ~> 4.60 | [registry.terraform.io](https://registry.terraform.io/providers/hashicorp/azurerm/latest) |
| Helm Provider | ~> 2.x | registry.terraform.io |
| Robusta | 0.10.30+ | robusta-charts.storage.googleapis.com |
| Mimir (distributed) | latest (v4.1+) | [grafana.github.io/helm-charts](https://grafana.com/docs/helm-charts/mimir-distributed/latest/) |
| Loki | 6.53.0 | grafana.github.io/helm-charts |
| Tempo (distributed) | 1.61.x | [grafana.github.io/helm-charts](https://artifacthub.io/packages/helm/grafana/tempo-distributed) |
| ArgoCD | Helm chart v9.4.x | [argoproj.github.io/argo-helm](https://artifacthub.io/packages/helm/argo/argo-cd) |
| Trivy Operator | v0.69.x | [aquasecurity/trivy](https://github.com/aquasecurity/trivy) |
| OTel Operator | latest | [open-telemetry/opentelemetry-operator](https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-operator) |
| Python | 3.13 | base Docker images |
